summary:
The Maadi culture was the principal Chalcolithic culture of Lower (northern) Egypt, flourishing approximately 3800–3300 BCE and centered on the site of Maadi near present-day Cairo. The Maadi culture is distinguished from its contemporaneous Upper Egyptian counterparts — the [[event:1634|Naqada II]] culture — by different pottery styles, burial practices, and settlement patterns. Maadi settlements were comparatively unplanned, consisting of oval pits used for storage, post-built structures, and subterranean chambers, contrasting with the more organized village layouts further south.

The Maadi people maintained active trading connections with the Levant, as evidenced by the presence of imported Canaanite pottery, copper ores, and bitumen at Maadi sites. They also had contact with Upper Egyptian Naqada communities, and the eventual political and cultural unification of Egypt is associated with the northward expansion of the [[event:1635|Naqada III]] cultural complex, which absorbed or replaced Maadi communities around 3300–3100 BCE. Maadi thus represents the northern Egyptian cultural tradition that was integrated into the unified Egyptian state under the early pharaohs.

read_more_links.title: Lower Egypt in Prehistory - Metropolitan Museum
